subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Prohibited Grounds and Social Areas
subtopic_heading: Human Rights Code Grounds — Prohibited Grounds and Social Areas
fact_pattern: An employer refuses to hire a qualified applicant after learning during a pre-employment medical examination that the applicant has type 1 diabetes. The employer claims the applicant could not safely perform the essential duties of the position without accommodation. The applicant has not yet been given an opportunity to demonstrate her ability to do the job with accommodation.
call_of_question: Under the Ontario Human Rights Code, which prohibited ground of discrimination is most directly engaged by the employer's refusal to hire, and in which social area?

options.A: Race discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of services.
options.B: Disability discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of employment.
options.C: Sex discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of employment.
options.D: Creed discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of vocational associations.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1), every person has a right to equal treatment with respect to employment without discrimination because of, inter alia, disability. Type 1 diabetes is a recognized disability within the meaning of the Code. The employer's refusal to hire the applicant on the basis of her diabetes — without first allowing her to demonstrate her ability to perform the job with accommodation — constitutes prima facie discrimination on the ground of disability in the area of employment under s. 5.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The fact pattern does not raise race discrimination; the ground engaged is disability. The social area is employment (s. 5), not services (s. 1).
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Disability discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of employment.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Sex is a separate prohibited ground under the Code; the fact pattern does not indicate sex-based discrimination. The employer's conduct relates solely to the applicant's medical condition (disability).
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Creed relates to religious belief, not medical conditions. Vocational associations is a separate social area under the Code not applicable to this pre-employment hiring scenario.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Human Rights Code Grounds — Prohibited Grounds and Social Areas
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1) (employment), s. 10 (definitions including disability)

tested_concepts: disability as prohibited ground under Code s. 5
employment as social area under Code s. 5(1)
prima facie discrimination

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Prohibited Grounds and Social Areas
fact_pattern: An employer's workplace policy requires all employees to work Friday evenings and Saturdays. A Jewish employee whose religious observance requires him to abstain from work from sunset Friday to nightfall Saturday asks for an exemption. The employer refuses, stating the policy applies equally to all employees and was not designed to target any religion.
call_of_question: Under the Human Rights Code, does a facially neutral employment policy that has an adverse effect on persons sharing a protected characteristic constitute prohibited discrimination?

options.A: No. A facially neutral policy that applies equally to all employees cannot constitute discrimination; there must be an intention to discriminate based on a prohibited ground.
options.B: Yes. Under s. 11 of the Code (constructive discrimination), a requirement that is not discriminatory on its face but results in adverse treatment of a group identified by a prohibited ground — here, creed — constitutes discrimination; the employer must accommodate the employee to the point of undue hardship.
options.C: No. Religious accommodation applies only where the employer specifically targeted the employee because of his religion; neutral policies with unintended effects are not within the scope of the Code.
options.D: Yes, but the employer has no obligation to accommodate unless the employee submits a written formal request for accommodation using the employer's specific HR procedures.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 11 (constructive discrimination), a requirement, qualification, or factor that is not explicitly discriminatory but has an adverse effect on a group of persons identified by a prohibited ground — such as creed — constitutes discrimination. The intent of the employer is irrelevant; adverse effect suffices. A rigid Saturday work requirement adversely impacts employees who observe the Jewish Sabbath, constituting constructive discrimination on the ground of creed. The employer is then required to accommodate to the point of undue hardship under s. 17.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Human rights law rejects intent as a prerequisite for discrimination; constructive (adverse effect) discrimination under s. 11 does not require proof of discriminatory intent.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes. Under s. 11 of the Code (constructive discrimination), a requirement that is not discriminatory on its face but results in adverse treatment of a group identified by a prohibited ground — here, creed — constitutes discrimination; the employer must accommodate the employee to the point of undue hardship.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Constructive discrimination under s. 11 does not require targeting; adverse effect on a protected group is sufficient regardless of the employer's intent.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to accommodate arises under the Code and does not depend on the employee following specific internal HR procedures; once a need is communicated, the duty to accommodate is engaged.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Prima Facie Discrimination — Constructive Discrimination
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 11 (constructive discrimination), s. 17 (undue hardship)

tested_concepts: s. 11 constructive discrimination
adverse effect discrimination no intent required
Saturday work requirement creed discrimination

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Duty to Accommodate
subtopic_heading: Duty to Accommodate — To the Point of Undue Hardship
fact_pattern: An employee with a back injury needs a modified work schedule and ergonomic equipment. The employer provides ergonomic equipment but refuses to modify the work schedule, arguing that any schedule modification would cause 'significant inconvenience' to customers and result in a small increase in labour costs. The employer argues that this constitutes undue hardship.
call_of_question: Under the Human Rights Code and the Meiorin test, does 'significant inconvenience' or minor increases in labour costs constitute undue hardship sufficient to excuse the employer from accommodating the employee?

options.A: Yes. Any financial cost or operational inconvenience to the employer satisfies the undue hardship defence; accommodation is only required where it is cost-free.
options.B: Yes. Customer inconvenience is always sufficient to establish undue hardship in a service-based industry.
options.C: No. Under the Human Rights Code s. 17 and the Meiorin test, undue hardship is a high threshold; relevant factors include health and safety considerations, cost (assessed in the context of the employer's overall financial capacity), and external sources of funding. Significant inconvenience to customers and minor cost increases, standing alone, do not meet the undue hardship threshold.
options.D: No. The employer must provide every accommodation requested without limitation; the concept of undue hardship has no application to employment accommodation.

explanation: The duty to accommodate to the point of undue hardship is established in British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, and codified in s. 17 of the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19. Undue hardship is a high standard; the factors relevant to the assessment include cost (taking into account the employer's overall financial capacity), health and safety, and availability of outside funding. Inconvenience, minor disruption, and small cost increases do not reach the threshold of undue hardship. The employer must demonstrate hardship that is truly unreasonable in the circumstances, not merely inconvenient.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Any cost or inconvenience does not suffice for undue hardship; the standard is specifically 'undue,' meaning the hardship must be substantial and not merely inconvenient.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Customer inconvenience alone does not constitute undue hardship; the assessment requires consideration of multiple factors in their totality, including the employer's overall capacity to accommodate.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No. Under the Human Rights Code s. 17 and the Meiorin test, undue hardship is a high threshold; relevant factors include health and safety considerations, cost (assessed in the context of the employer's overall financial capacity), and external sources of funding. Significant inconvenience to customers and minor cost increases, standing alone, do not meet the undue hardship threshold.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to accommodate has a defined outer limit — undue hardship — which is part of the Code; the duty is not unlimited.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Duty to Accommodate — To the Point of Undue Hardship
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17; British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3

tested_concepts: undue hardship high threshold
Meiorin test factors cost health safety
inconvenience insufficient for undue hardship

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Duty to Accommodate
subtopic_heading: Duty to Accommodate — Meiorin Unified Test
fact_pattern: A fire department has a physical fitness standard requiring all firefighters to run 2.5 km in 11 minutes. A qualified female firefighter passes all other aspects of her annual fitness evaluation but consistently fails to meet this particular running standard despite her best efforts. Statistical evidence shows women on average require more time to complete this distance than men. The department threatens to terminate her employment.
call_of_question: Under the unified approach to the duty to accommodate established in Meiorin, what must the employer demonstrate to justify the standard as a bona fide occupational requirement (BFOR)?

options.A: The employer must prove only that the standard was adopted for safety reasons; once safety is established, no further justification is needed.
options.B: Under the Meiorin unified three-part BFOR test, the employer must demonstrate: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it is necessary for the fulfilment of that legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish that purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the employee without incurring undue hardship.
options.C: The employer must prove only that the standard applies equally to all employees; equal application alone satisfies the BFOR defence.
options.D: The Meiorin test does not apply to physical fitness standards; those standards are evaluated under a separate legislative regime for safety-critical occupations.

explanation: In British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the Supreme Court of Canada unified the previously separate direct and adverse effect discrimination tests and established a three-part BFOR test for discriminatory standards. The employer must prove: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to job performance; (2) the standard was adopted in good faith belief it was necessary; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary — meaning individual accommodation to the point of undue hardship is not possible. A running standard that has an adverse effect on women must be justified under all three parts of this test, including showing that no accommodation short of undue hardship is possible.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Safety rationale alone does not satisfy the Meiorin BFOR test; the employer must also demonstrate that the standard cannot be modified to accommodate without undue hardship (the third Meiorin element).
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under the Meiorin unified three-part BFOR test, the employer must demonstrate: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it is necessary for the fulfilment of that legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish that purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the employee without incurring undue hardship.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Equal application of a neutral standard is precisely the scenario Meiorin's unified test was designed to address; equal application does not constitute a BFOR defence if the standard has adverse discriminatory effect.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The Meiorin test applies broadly to all employment standards, including physical fitness standards in safety-critical occupations; there is no separate legislative regime that excludes these from the Meiorin analysis.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Duty to Accommodate — Meiorin Unified Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17; British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3

tested_concepts: Meiorin three-part BFOR test
unified test for direct and adverse effect discrimination
reasonably necessary accommodation element

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: HRTO Process — Complaint, Mediation, Hearing, and s. 45.1 Dismissal
fact_pattern: A complainant files an application at the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) alleging sexual harassment by her manager over a two-year period. The HRTO notifies the parties and offers mediation. The employer's counsel contacts the HRTO and argues the application should be dismissed summarily because the applicant's complaint is frivolous, having no reasonable prospect of success, and was filed purely to harm the reputation of the manager.
call_of_question: Under s. 45.1 of the Human Rights Code, on what grounds may the HRTO dismiss an application without a full hearing?

options.A: The HRTO may dismiss any application where the respondent demonstrates the applicant has filed more than one previous complaint.
options.B: Under s. 45.1 of the Code, the HRTO may dismiss an application, in whole or in part, if the Tribunal is of the opinion that the application or part is frivolous, vexatious, or made in bad faith; or where the Code does not apply; or where the application has already been appropriately dealt with.
options.C: The HRTO may dismiss an application only after a full merits hearing; there is no summary dismissal power under the Code.
options.D: The HRTO may dismiss an application at any time simply if the respondent provides an affidavit denying the allegations.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.1, the HRTO may dismiss an application without a hearing on the merits if the Tribunal is of the opinion that the application is frivolous, vexatious, or made in bad faith; the Code does not apply to the claim; another proceeding has appropriately dealt with the substance of the application; or where the application was filed past applicable time limits. This summary dismissal power allows the HRTO to screen applications efficiently without requiring a full hearing in cases where the application clearly lacks merit.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The number of previous complaints filed by the applicant is not a ground for dismissal under s. 45.1; the grounds are frivolous, vexatious, bad faith, Code inapplicability, or prior proceedings.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 45.1 of the Code, the HRTO may dismiss an application, in whole or in part, if the Tribunal is of the opinion that the application or part is frivolous, vexatious, or made in bad faith; or where the Code does not apply; or where the application has already been appropriately dealt with.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 45.1 expressly grants the HRTO a summary dismissal power without a full hearing; the HRTO is not required to conduct a full merits hearing in every case before dismissing.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A respondent's affidavit denying the allegations does not automatically entitle the respondent to dismissal; the HRTO must form the opinion that one of the s. 45.1 grounds is met.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: HRTO Process — Complaint, Mediation, Hearing, and s. 45.1 Dismissal
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.1

tested_concepts: HRTO s. 45.1 dismissal grounds
frivolous vexatious bad faith
summary dismissal without full hearing

subject: public_law
fact_pattern: The Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario finds that an employee was terminated from her employment because of her pregnancy, contrary to s. 5 of the Human Rights Code. The Tribunal awards monetary compensation. The employee argues that in addition to monetary compensation for lost wages, she should also receive compensation for injury to her dignity, feelings, and self-respect. The employer argues the HRTO can only award lost wages and cannot award non-economic damages.
call_of_question: Under s. 45.2 of the Human Rights Code, what remedies may the HRTO award following a finding of discrimination?

options.A: The HRTO may award only monetary compensation for past and future lost wages; it has no power to award non-economic damages.
options.B: Under s. 45.2 of the Code, the HRTO may award monetary compensation for: (i) lost income and any other compensation monetarily quantifiable; and (ii) injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect (non-economic general damages). The Tribunal may also make orders requiring the respondent to do or refrain from doing something.
options.C: The HRTO may award punitive damages as its primary remedy to deter future discrimination.
options.D: The HRTO has no remedial power; it can only issue declarations of discrimination, leaving monetary relief to the courts.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.2, the HRTO may, on finding a contravention, order the respondent to pay monetary compensation to the applicant for lost income and other quantifiable losses, and to compensate the applicant for injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect. Section 45.2 also empowers the HRTO to make orders that the respondent do or refrain from doing anything to implement the Code's purposes. The HRTO does not award punitive damages in the traditional sense, though large awards for injury to dignity may serve a general deterrence function.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 45.2 expressly authorizes the HRTO to award compensation for injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect — a form of non-economic general damages — in addition to monetary compensation for lost wages.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 45.2 of the Code, the HRTO may award monetary compensation for: (i) lost income and any other compensation monetarily quantifiable; and (ii) injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect (non-economic general damages). The Tribunal may also make orders requiring the respondent to do or refrain from doing something.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The HRTO does not award punitive damages as its primary remedy; the Code's remedial scheme focuses on compensating the applicant (lost income and injury to dignity) and systemic remedies (orders to do or refrain from doing something).
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 45.2 expressly grants the HRTO broad remedial power including monetary awards and systemic orders; the HRTO is not limited to declaratory relief.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: HRTO Remedies — Section 45.2
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.2

tested_concepts: HRTO s. 45.2 remedial powers
monetary compensation lost wages and injury to dignity
orders to do or refrain from doing

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Settlement at HRTO
fact_pattern: Following mediation at the HRTO, a complainant and respondent reach a written settlement agreement. The respondent agrees to pay the complainant $25,000 and to implement a workplace anti-harassment training program within 90 days. The HRTO approves the settlement. Four months later, the respondent has paid the money but has not implemented the training program. The complainant asks the HRTO to enforce the settlement.
call_of_question: Under the Human Rights Code, how may a party enforce a settlement reached at the HRTO that the other party has partially breached?

options.A: The complainant must commence a new application at the HRTO re-alleging the original discrimination; the settlement agreement cannot itself be enforced at the HRTO.
options.B: The complainant must bring an action in the Superior Court of Justice to enforce the settlement agreement as a contract.
options.C: Under s. 45 of the Code, a settlement agreement may be filed with the HRTO, which then becomes an order of the Tribunal; an order of the HRTO is enforceable in the same manner as an order of the Superior Court of Justice, allowing enforcement through the court's contempt or enforcement mechanisms.
options.D: A settlement reached at the HRTO is not legally binding; either party may unilaterally resile from the agreement at any time.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45, the Tribunal may make an order giving effect to a settlement. Once filed and made into a Tribunal order, the order has the same force and effect as a Tribunal order made after a hearing and may be filed in the Superior Court of Justice for enforcement purposes. This mechanism allows the aggrieved party to enforce a breached settlement through the court's enforcement machinery (including writs of execution and contempt proceedings) without having to re-litigate the underlying human rights complaint.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The complainant is not required to re-file a new application alleging original discrimination; the settlement itself, once made into an order of the HRTO, can be directly enforced.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. While a breach of a settlement agreement might theoretically support a contract claim, the Code provides a specific enforcement mechanism through the HRTO's order-making power; this is the more appropriate and direct remedy.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 45 of the Code, a settlement agreement may be filed with the HRTO, which then becomes an order of the Tribunal; an order of the HRTO is enforceable in the same manner as an order of the Superior Court of Justice, allowing enforcement through the court's contempt or enforcement mechanisms.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. HRTO-approved settlements are legally binding; once the HRTO approves the settlement and makes it an order, neither party can unilaterally resile from the agreement.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Code Section 46 — Settlement Agreement Enforcement
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45 (settlement), s. 45.2 (remedies)

tested_concepts: HRTO settlement made into order s. 45
enforcement as Superior Court order
settlement binding once approved

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Duty to Accommodate
subtopic_heading: Undue Hardship — Cost, Health, and Safety Factors
fact_pattern: An employee with a chronic back condition requires a height-adjustable desk and the right to take short standing breaks every 30 minutes. The employer argues that providing an adjustable desk would cost $1,200 and that allowing standing breaks would disrupt production flow. The employer refuses accommodation, stating the cost and disruption constitute undue hardship. The employee files a Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario complaint.
call_of_question: Under the Ontario Human Rights Code, what factors does the Tribunal consider in determining whether an employer has established undue hardship, and does the evidence described likely establish undue hardship?

options.A: Undue hardship is established whenever the requested accommodation involves any additional cost to the employer; a $1,200 cost automatically constitutes undue hardship.
options.B: Under the Code and the Ontario Human Rights Commission Policy on Disability and the Duty to Accommodate, the three recognized factors for undue hardship are: (1) cost, (2) outside sources of funding, and (3) health and safety. The cost of accommodation is assessed relative to the employer's overall resources; a $1,200 cost for a large employer would very likely not constitute undue hardship. Minor production disruption does not meet the health and safety threshold for undue hardship.
options.C: An employer establishes undue hardship by showing that any accommodation would reduce productivity; production disruption is sufficient to establish undue hardship without further analysis.
options.D: Undue hardship can be established by proving the accommodation would be inconvenient or would require any modification to existing workplace practices.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(2), and as articulated in Meiorin (BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU, [1999] 3 SCR 3) and the OHRC Policy on Disability, the duty to accommodate requires employers to accommodate to the point of undue hardship. Undue hardship is assessed by reference to three defined factors: (1) cost — whether the financial cost of accommodation would fundamentally alter the nature of the enterprise; (2) outside sources of funding — whether the employer has access to external financial support; and (3) health and safety risks — whether accommodation would create unacceptable safety risks. Mere inconvenience, reduced efficiency, or minor production adjustment do not constitute undue hardship. A $1,200 expenditure, absent evidence it is disproportionate to the employer's resources, would rarely establish undue hardship.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Undue hardship is not established by any additional cost; the cost must be so significant relative to the employer's resources as to fundamentally alter the enterprise. A nominal cost of $1,200 would not establish undue hardship for most employers.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under the Code and the Ontario Human Rights Commission Policy on Disability and the Duty to Accommodate, the three recognized factors for undue hardship are: (1) cost, (2) outside sources of funding, and (3) health and safety. The cost of accommodation is assessed relative to the employer's overall resources; a $1,200 cost for a large employer would very likely not constitute undue hardship. Minor production disruption does not meet the health and safety threshold for undue hardship.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Production disruption alone does not constitute undue hardship; the health and safety factor requires an actual and significant safety risk, not merely a reduction in productivity or production flow.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Inconvenience and modification to existing practices are part of what accommodation necessarily entails; they do not, by themselves, constitute undue hardship. The standard is 'undue' hardship — a high threshold.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Undue Hardship — Cost, Health, and Safety Factors
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(2); BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3

tested_concepts: three factors of undue hardship cost outside funding health safety
cost assessed relative to employer resources
inconvenience not undue hardship

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Human Rights Code — Prima Facie Discrimination
subtopic_heading: Prima Facie Discrimination — Onus Shift to Respondent
fact_pattern: Amara, an Indigenous woman, applies for a senior project manager position at a large construction company. The hiring panel gives each candidate a composite score based on four criteria: technical qualifications (30%), interview performance (30%), cultural fit as assessed by panelists (20%), and seniority within the company (20%). Amara scores highest on technical qualifications and seniority (she has been with the company 11 years). She scores mid-range on interview performance. However, the panel rates her low on 'cultural fit,' noting she 'seemed uncomfortable with our team culture' and 'might have difficulty integrating with the existing group.' The position is awarded to a less-senior non-Indigenous candidate with lower technical qualifications but higher cultural fit ratings. Amara files a human rights complaint alleging race discrimination in the promotion decision. The employer argues: (i) the technical and seniority factors alone do not establish discrimination because the composite score lawfully weighs multiple criteria; (ii) cultural fit is a legitimate business consideration; and (iii) even if Amara's Indigenous identity was a subconscious factor in the cultural fit assessment, a prima facie case requires showing that race was the dominant or decisive reason for the decision, not merely one among many.
call_of_question: Applying the prima facie discrimination test from Moore v British Columbia (Education), which of the following best describes whether Amara has established a prima facie case?

options.A: Amara has not established a prima facie case because the employer applied a composite scoring system with four criteria; where a decision-maker considers multiple factors and the protected characteristic was only one factor, the applicant must prove the protected characteristic was the dominant or decisive reason before the burden shifts.
options.B: Amara has not established a prima facie case because 'cultural fit' is a recognized legitimate business criterion; a facially neutral criterion is inherently non-discriminatory and cannot form part of a prima facie case regardless of how it is applied.
options.C: Amara has established a prima facie case. Under Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61, a prima facie case of discrimination requires showing: (1) a characteristic protected by the Code; (2) an adverse impact; and (3) that the protected characteristic was a factor in the adverse treatment — not the dominant or sole factor. Where a facially neutral criterion like 'cultural fit' was assessed in a manner that may reflect racial stereotyping or group assumptions, and the complainant holds a protected characteristic, the connection between the characteristic and the adverse treatment is established sufficiently to shift the evidential burden.
options.D: Amara cannot rely on the prima facie discrimination framework at all because the Human Rights Code requires proof of intentional discrimination; where the employer did not deliberately exclude Amara because she is Indigenous, no Code violation can be established.

explanation: Under Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61, and the Ontario Human Rights Tribunal's prima facie discrimination framework, a prima facie case requires proof on a balance of probabilities that: (1) the complainant has a characteristic protected by the Code (Indigenous identity is a protected ground under 'race' and 'ancestry'); (2) the complainant experienced an adverse impact (denial of promotion); and (3) the protected characteristic was a factor in the adverse treatment. The protected characteristic does not need to be the dominant, decisive, or sole reason for the adverse treatment — it is sufficient that it was a contributing factor. The employer's argument that discrimination requires a dominant or decisive causal role misstates the law. Here, the low 'cultural fit' rating — assessed by language suggesting discomfort with group integration that may reflect racial stereotyping or unconscious bias — combined with Amara's objectively stronger performance on quantifiable criteria and her Indigenous identity, provides sufficient basis for a prima facie case. The burden then shifts to the employer to provide a credible, non-discriminatory explanation for the cultural fit ratings and the resulting decision.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. This is a General Rule Trap that overstates the causation threshold. The prima facie discrimination test does not require the protected characteristic to be the dominant or decisive reason for the adverse treatment. It is sufficient that race was a contributing factor. Multi-factorial decision-making does not insulate a respondent from a prima facie case; the question is whether the protected characteristic was present in the causal mix, not whether it outweighed all other factors.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. This is a Pragmatic Bluff. 'Cultural fit' is not inherently immune from human rights scrutiny simply because it is framed as a neutral business criterion. A facially neutral criterion can operate in a discriminatory manner in its application — for example, if panelists' assessment of 'cultural fit' is influenced by racial stereotypes or group assumptions about Indigenous employees. The neutral label does not insulate the criterion from prima facie analysis.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Amara has established a prima facie case. Under Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61, a prima facie case of discrimination requires showing: (1) a characteristic protected by the Code; (2) an adverse impact; and (3) that the protected characteristic was a factor in the adverse treatment — not the dominant or sole factor. Where a facially neutral criterion like 'cultural fit' was assessed in a manner that may reflect racial stereotyping or group assumptions, and the complainant holds a protected characteristic, the connection between the characteristic and the adverse treatment is established sufficiently to shift the evidential burden.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. This is an Overstatement Trap. The Ontario Human Rights Code prohibits both intentional (direct) and adverse effects discrimination. Proof of deliberate or conscious intent to discriminate is not required. Systemic, structural, and unconscious discrimination — including where a facially neutral criterion is applied in a manner influenced by stereotypical assumptions — falls within the Code's prohibition. The absence of explicit discriminatory intent does not prevent a prima facie case from being established.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Prima Facie Discrimination — Onus Shift to Respondent
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1); Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61

tested_concepts: prima facie case — protected characteristic need only be a contributing factor not dominant or sole cause
multi-factor decision-making does not require dominant causal role to establish prima facie case
facially neutral criterion — cultural fit — can support prima facie case if applied with discriminatory effect
intentional discrimination not required — adverse effects and unconscious bias covered by Code

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: Reprisal Protection — Section 8 of the Human Rights Code
fact_pattern: An employee files a discrimination complaint against her employer with the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario. Two weeks after filing the complaint, her employer gives her a negative performance review, reduces her hours, and moves her to a less desirable shift — conduct she had not experienced before filing the complaint. She alleges reprisal. The employer argues the performance review was justified and any coincidence in timing is irrelevant.
call_of_question: Under s. 8 of the Ontario Human Rights Code, what constitutes prohibited reprisal, and what must be established to make out a reprisal claim?

options.A: Reprisal under s. 8 requires proof that the employer expressly stated it was retaliating for the complaint; adverse treatment without an express statement of retaliatory intent does not constitute reprisal.
options.B: Section 8 prohibits reprisal only where the complaint is ultimately found to be meritorious; if the underlying discrimination complaint fails, the reprisal claim also fails.
options.C: Under s. 8 of the Human Rights Code, reprisal is prohibited where a person engages in a protected activity (such as filing a human rights complaint) and subsequently experiences adverse treatment that is causally connected to that protected activity; an adverse inference may be drawn from close temporal proximity between the protected activity and the adverse treatment, and the applicant need not prove discriminatory intent — only that the protected activity was a factor in the adverse treatment.
options.D: Reprisal under s. 8 applies only to dismissal from employment; lesser adverse actions such as shift changes and reduced hours are not captured by the reprisal protection.

explanation: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 8, it is prohibited to threaten or penalize a person for claiming or enforcing a right under the Code or for participating in a Code proceeding. A reprisal claim requires: (1) a protected activity (filing a human rights complaint); (2) adverse treatment by the respondent; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse treatment. An express statement of retaliatory intent is not required; close temporal proximity between the protected activity and the adverse treatment — as here, where adverse actions began two weeks after the complaint was filed — can support an inference of reprisal. The validity of the underlying complaint is irrelevant to the reprisal claim.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Reprisal under s. 8 does not require an express statement of retaliatory intent; circumstantial evidence including timing and pattern of conduct can establish the causal connection.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. A reprisal claim stands independently of the underlying discrimination complaint; reprisal protection applies even if the underlying complaint does not succeed on its merits.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 8 of the Human Rights Code, reprisal is prohibited where a person engages in a protected activity (such as filing a human rights complaint) and subsequently experiences adverse treatment that is causally connected to that protected activity; an adverse inference may be drawn from close temporal proximity between the protected activity and the adverse treatment, and the applicant need not prove discriminatory intent — only that the protected activity was a factor in the adverse treatment.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 8 reprisal protection is not limited to dismissal; it covers any adverse treatment — including negative performance reviews, reduction of hours, and disadvantageous shift changes — that is causally connected to a protected activity.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: Reprisal Protection — Section 8 of the Human Rights Code
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 8

tested_concepts: s. 8 reprisal protection elements
temporal proximity supports causal inference
reprisal claim independent of underlying complaint success

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: BFOR — Bona Fide Occupational Requirement and the Meiorin Test
fact_pattern: A wildfire-fighting organization requires all firefighters to pass an aerobic capacity test demonstrating the ability to carry a 45-kilogram pack and run 2.5 kilometres within a specific time. A female applicant fails the test and is denied employment. She argues the standard discriminates on the basis of sex because fewer women can pass it. The employer argues the standard is a bona fide occupational requirement necessary to fight wildfires safely.
call_of_question: Under the Meiorin test, what must an employer demonstrate to establish that a standard constituting prima facie discrimination is a bona fide occupational requirement?

options.A: The employer must show the standard was designed in good faith; the discriminatory effect on a protected group does not need to be justified.
options.B: The employer must show the standard is rationally connected to the job — that it tests for skills actually needed. No further justification is required.
options.C: Under BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the employer must establish on a balance of probabilities: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it was necessary to fulfill the legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish the legitimate work-related purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the affected person without incurring undue hardship.
options.D: An employer establishes a BFOR by demonstrating that the majority of employees in the same position can meet the standard; if most people pass, it is a valid requirement.

explanation: In BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the Supreme Court of Canada established the unified three-part BFOR test. To establish a bona fide occupational requirement, the employer must prove on a balance of probabilities that: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job — not the discriminatory purpose itself; (2) it was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that the purpose was legitimately necessary; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish the legitimate work-related purpose — that it is impossible to accommodate persons who fail the standard without incurring undue hardship. Critically, the employer must also show it made efforts to craft a standard that accommodates difference, including sex-based physiological differences, to the point of undue hardship.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Good faith in adopting the standard is only the second step of the Meiorin test; establishing good faith alone is not sufficient. The employer must also demonstrate rational connection and reasonable necessity.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Rational connection is only the first step of the Meiorin test; the employer must also satisfy good faith and reasonable necessity, including demonstrating that accommodation of those who cannot meet the standard would cause undue hardship.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the employer must establish on a balance of probabilities: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it was necessary to fulfill the legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish the legitimate work-related purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the affected person without incurring undue hardship.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The Meiorin test does not ask whether the majority of workers can meet the standard; a standard that systematically excludes a protected group must be justified through the three-part test, regardless of what percentage of people in a different group can pass it.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: BFOR — Bona Fide Occupational Requirement and the Meiorin Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(1); BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3

exam_trigger_words: accommodation to point of undue hardship
tested_concepts: Meiorin three-part BFOR test
rational connection good faith reasonable necessity
accommodation integral to BFOR third step
